claimed_contribution: The paper proposes a multiple ideal point model (MIPM) that allows each household or individual to have multiple ideal points, with one activated probabilistically at each purchase occasion.

It introduces an estimation procedure for internal or external preference analysis using household product-switching matrices from scanner-panel data, with Markov transitions among ideal points and CAIC-based selection of the number of ideal points.
It provides simulation and empirical evidence to assess whether the model can recover underlying choice structure and yield managerially useful positioning and segmentation insights.
context: The empirical setting is the powdered soft drink category in consumer packaged goods. The application uses Information Resources Inc. household scanner-panel purchases from five U.S. markets (Pittsfield MA, Marion IN, Eau Claire WI, Midland TX, and Rome GA).
design_and_data: The authors formulate household choice probabilities as a function of distances from products to potentially multiple ideal points, and model transitions between active ideal points with a first-order Markov matrix (with zero-order as a restricted case). Parameters are estimated by iterative maximum likelihood, alternating household-level ideal point and transition parameters with market-level product locations under homogeneous perceptions and heterogeneous preferences; CAIC is used to choose the number of ideal points per household. They run exploratory full-factorial simulations varying number of consumers, product dimensions, products, purchase string length, and number of ideal points to evaluate parameter recovery. Empirically, they analyze IRI panel data: after screening, 59 households, 5826 choices, seven flavors, purchase strings of 32-307, with 2/3 estimation and 1/3 holdout validation; MIPM is compared with single ideal point and wandering ideal point models using Bayesian cross-validated likelihood.
main_findings: In holdout validation, the MIPM outperformed both comparison models, with total log-likelihood -6498.33 versus -6787.40 for the single ideal point model and -7161.38 for the wandering ideal point model.
CAIC selected multiple ideal points for most households: 48% were assigned two ideal points and 18% three ideal points, while 34% were assigned one ideal point.
Transition-order tests showed heterogeneity in dynamics: 63% of households were adequately modeled as zero-order, but 37% exhibited higher-order ideal-point transition behavior.
one_line_summary: The paper develops and validates a household-level multiple ideal point model using scanner-panel switching data and shows that it captures choice behavior better than single-ideal and wandering-ideal alternatives in powdered soft drinks.
relation_to_prior_work: extends ideal point modeling by replacing the single invariant preference assumption with a within-household set of latent ideal points that are probabilistically activated (and can follow Markov transitions), while keeping the map-based interpretation used in positioning research.
research_gap: Prior ideal point models typically assume a single stable preference, which can be misleading when household choices reflect multiple users, usage occasions, or variety seeking. Existing alternatives either require a priori specification of usage contexts or do not provide household-level, map-based analysis from standard scanner-panel switching data.
research_question: Can household choice behavior be represented as switching among multiple latent ideal points rather than a single invariant ideal point? If so, can a model using product-switching matrices recover this structure and improve fit and prediction relative to single ideal point and wandering ideal point models?
